problem_summary: 2006 MCM/ICM Problem C (interdisciplinary) tackled the HIV/AIDS pandemic: teams had to model the spread of HIV through ~2050 and evaluate how three intervention levers — sexual education / condom use, antiretroviral therapy (ARV), and a hypothetical HIV vaccine — would change the trajectory, then translate this into resource-allocation policy advice (a UN "white paper"). Team 787 (Duke) built a custom gender-stratified, discrete-time iterative deterministic compartmental model of the HIV+ population driven by the epidemiological reproductive number R0, calibrated it against 1995–2005 South African antenatal-clinic prevalence data, ran it across five archetype countries (South Africa, India, Russia, USA, Australia) chosen to span distinct transmission profiles, performed sensitivity analysis on key parameters, and capped it with a cost-benefit / discounted-value economic model for ARV distribution plus a policy white paper.
models_used: Discrete-time iterative deterministic compartmental model: #aids(t) = newInfections(t) + #aids(t-1) - deaths(t), stepped yearly with t0 = year 2000 and negative indices seeded from historical data
R0 (basic reproductive number) framing as the conceptual backbone — epidemic grows iff R0>1, and a 'treatment' only counts as preventative if it lowers R0 (cites Velasco-Hernandez 2002)

Gender-stratified force-of-infection / mass-action transmission term for heterosexual sex: intercourseT(t) = rho * riskSexCons * (1 - condomRate) * #aids_oppositeGender(t-1) * (1 - %aids_susceptibleGender), with male->female transmission set 2x female->male

Separate additive transmission channels: needle/IV-drug term needleT(t)=rho_d*riskDrugCons*#HIV-negativeDrugUsers*0.35*%HIV+drug (80/20 male/female split, 35% dirty-needle rate); mother-to-child birthT(t)=birthRate*#aids_women(t-1)*riskBirth (1-5% first world, up to 35% undeveloped); a homosexual-intercourse term with its own per-act risk constant (used mainly for the USA)
Fixed-delay feedback death model: deaths(t) = newInfections(t - averageDeath) with averageDeath ~9 years (no age tracking; everyone assumed to die of AIDS)

ARV sub-model via two tunable input parameters: arvFactor in [0.25, 1] (multiplier on transmission of treated patients, encoding the unresolved literature debate on whether ARV reduces transmission) and arvPortion (fraction treated); newInfections split into treated vs untreated cohorts

Drug-resistance (MDR) sub-model: newInfections_resistant(t) = %onARV * (1 - adherenceRate) * chanceToMutate, with resistant carriers tracked separately so they no longer benefit from ARV (adherence 85-95%)

Logistic adoption curve for vaccine rollout: %vaccinated grows logistically from 0% (starting 2015) to a country-specific steady state proxied by the WHO tetanus-toxoid 2nd-dose coverage rate; susceptible term becomes (1 - %aids - %vaccinated)
Five-criterion qualitative country-selection scoring framework (prevalence trend, infected demographic, education/awareness level, transmission routes, data integrity/availability)

Economic cost-benefit model with discounting: Cost_{p,t} = sum_{t=1..n} 1100p/(1+r)^{t-1} (present value of ARV cost at $1100/person/yr); Savings = GDP_{p,t} + netExternalities_{p,t} - GDP_t; NetBenefit = Savings - Cost; optimum found where partial derivatives wrt p and t equal zero, then matched against rich-country investment maturities
key_techniques: Calibration / historical back-fitting against the most reliable available dataset (South African antenatal prevalence 1995-2005) before any forecasting, tuning only 3 parameters (lifespan 9->7, risky-sex constant up, rising condom rate) — and showing the fit table

Encoding scientific uncertainty as a tunable parameter rather than picking a side: arvFactor spans the full range of the contradictory ARV-transmission literature (Anderson 1992 vs Krieger 1991 vs Royce 1997), so the model reports BOTH outcomes

Archetype/representative-case selection: one country per continent each isolating a different dominant transmission vector (SA = heterosexual/no drugs, Russia = IV drug use, USA = MSM/homosexual, India = scale + mixed, Australia = imported cases) to demonstrate generality without modeling every nation

Systematic per-section assumption ledgers with explicit 'worst-case scenario' justification (static condom rate, 100% condom efficacy, all carriers die of AIDS, fixed 9-yr delay) — pre-empting judge objections

One-factor-at-a-time sensitivity analysis on condomRate (0/30/50/100%), arvPortion (0/20/50%) under both arvFactor regimes, and MDR adherence (85/95/100%), with a sanity check that each response matches intuition

Self-flagging of model artifacts: explicitly calls out the ~2020 spike as an artifact of the instantaneous-lifespan-extension assumption, and notes the 100%-condom plateau not reaching zero is due to death-rate rounding in early iterations

Layering an economic/policy layer (discounted cost-benefit, positive/negative externalities including cross-border infection) on top of the epidemiological model to directly answer the resource-allocation question
Narrative framing device ('Quest for the Holy Grail of AIDS') and a stand-alone UN white paper translating math into actionable, tiered policy recommendations
why_it_won: "From a judge's seat, this paper wins on completeness, honesty, and decision-relevance rather than mathematical sophistication. (1) It answers the actual question asked — it doesn't just forecast HIV, it ranks the three interventions and outputs a tiered, country-segmented policy white paper plus an economic allocation rule, which is exactly what Problem C demanded. (2) It validates before it predicts: back-fitting to real South African antenatal data with a visible fit table gives the forecasts credibility, something most teams skip. (3) It handles genuine scientific controversy maturely — instead of hand-waving the unresolved ARV-transmission debate, it parameterizes it (arvFactor) and reports the counterintuitive but defensible result that lifespan-extending-only ARV can make an epidemic WORSE; this kind of nuanced, non-obvious finding is catnip to judges. (4) It is radically transparent about its own weaknesses — naming the 2020 spike and the non-zero plateau as artifacts shows scientific maturity and disarms reviewers. (5) Breadth: it integrates five transmission channels, drug resistance, a logistic vaccine rollout, five real countries, sensitivity analysis, and economics into one coherent, scalable framework. (6) Strong communication — memorable title, clean structure, abstract that states concrete conclusions, and policy prose a non-mathematician UN reader could act on."
section_highlights: "Abstract: concrete and conclusion-first — states the model type, lists which levers matter, and commits to specific findings (condoms work and SA is plateauing; ARV gets a 'mixed review' and may backfire; vaccine is the long-term answer). Section 2 (Experimental/Model): exemplary build-up from a single-channel basic model to the full multi-vector model, each parameter introduced with its data source citation AND a boxed list of assumptions with worst-case justification. Section 2.4 (Country Choice): an explicit five-indicator selection rubric — turns an arbitrary choice into a defensible methodology. Section 3.1 (Historical Fitting): the credibility anchor — a side-by-side historical-vs-predicted table for South Africa 1995-2005. Section 4 (Analysis): country-by-country interpretation that surfaces the headline insight (transmission-neutral ARV is catastrophic except in the MSM-dominated US) and reads the differences mechanistically. Section 4.1 (Sensitivity): clean OFAT sweeps over condom rate, ARV portion, and MDR adherence with intuition checks. Section 5 (Economic Model): discounted cost-benefit with externalities and a first-order optimality condition, honestly flagged as abstract due to missing data. Section 6.1 (White Paper): a genuinely separate, policy-audience deliverable with tiered country-prioritization rules. Section 6.2: an honest strengths/weaknesses ledger."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The published chart data is badly garbled in this markdown extraction (e.g. Figure 4/5 tables show nonsense like '1156227', '2e-6', '1.4E+7' year offsets) — the underlying figures presumably were fine, but it's a reminder that quantitative results live in plots that can be lost; the prose conclusions don't always let a reader reconstruct the numbers

Self-admitted modeling artifact: the instantaneous, population-wide lifespan extension when ARV 'switches on' (~2015-2020) produces a non-physical spike in cases around 2020 — a discretization/step-function artifact rather than real dynamics

Crude death model: a single fixed delay (everyone dies exactly averageDeath=9 years after infection, and everyone is assumed to die of AIDS) ignores age structure, individual survival variance, and non-AIDS mortality — the team acknowledges this
Countries modeled as isolated, closed populations with no migration — a real weakness given that the paper's own economic section hinges on cross-border (imported) infection in Australia/USA

The headline economic model is entirely abstract — it specifies Cost/Savings/NetBenefit and a first-order optimality condition but never plugs in GDP or externality data, so it yields no actual numbers or recommended (p*, t*)

Several strong simplifying assumptions (condoms 100% effective, vaccine 100% effective, constant 35% dirty-needle rate, static condom usage) are defended as 'worst case' but stack up and are not independently stress-tested together
Vaccine steady-state coverage is proxied by tetanus-toxoid 2nd-dose rates — a clever stand-in but an unvalidated analogy for HIV-vaccine uptake behavior
Sensitivity analysis is one-factor-at-a-time only; no joint/interaction or Monte Carlo uncertainty quantification, and no confidence bands on the 2050 forecasts
The model has many hand-set parameters (risk constants, rho=160-200 acts/yr, 80/20 drug split) drawn from heterogeneous sources; no formal uncertainty propagation from those inputs to outputs
reusable_patterns: Discrete-time state-update template: State(t) = inflows(t) + State(t-1) - outflows(t), seeded with historical data at negative indices — a simple, judge-legible alternative to ODE compartment models that is trivial to code and extend

Parameterize controversy: when the literature disagrees on a coefficient, make it an input parameter spanning the disputed range and report results across the whole range instead of choosing one value
Validate-then-forecast: always back-fit the model to the single most reliable historical dataset you can find and SHOW the fit table/plot before presenting any forward prediction
Per-section assumption ledger with 'worst-case' framing: after each sub-model, list every assumption and argue why it is conservative/bounds the answer — pre-empts the most common judge criticism

Archetype case selection with an explicit scoring rubric: instead of modeling all entities, pick a small set of representative cases that each isolate one dominant mechanism, and justify the choice with a stated multi-criterion framework

Additive, modular transmission channels: build a basic single-channel model first, then add independent terms (needles, births, MSM, vaccine, resistance) each gated by its own toggle parameter so scenarios switch on/off cleanly — gives easy ablation and sensitivity sweeps
Self-audit of artifacts: proactively identify any non-physical features of your output (spikes, non-zero plateaus) and attribute them to specific assumptions — turns a flaw into evidence of rigor
OFAT sensitivity-with-intuition-check: sweep each key parameter over a few values and explicitly confirm the response direction matches physical intuition

Cap a technical model with a decision layer: add an economic cost-benefit/discounted-value model and a non-technical 'white paper' that converts the math into tiered, actionable recommendations for the stated stakeholder (here, the UN)
Use a memorable framing/title and a conclusion-first abstract to make the paper stick with tired judges
